# UC-19.2.25 · Nutanix Cluster Health Score and Critical Services

> **Criticality:** Critical &middot; **Difficulty:** Intermediate &middot; **Pillar:** Observability &middot; **Type:** Availability, Reliability &middot; **Wave:** Crawl &middot; **Status:** Verified

*We watch one health number for each Nutanix block your company already depends on, and we check whether the small programs that keep the shared storage and the catalog of where data lives are really up. If the number falls into a bad band, or a truly important program is not up, the right contact is told in time, while a planned quiet week can still hush the noise if the case is not an emergency..*

---

## Description

Ingests Prism Element cluster MO on `nutanix:cluster`, where the vendor exposes a 0–100 cluster health score (not the same as the Prism Central /v3 alert stream) plus redundancy metadata, and juxtaposes that with per–CVM service_name and UP/DOWN/NA rows from `nutanix:cluster:services_status` (or a rare TA build that tucks `service_name` / `service_status` on `nutanix:cluster` events) so a single result row answers two questions: is the composite score in a good band, and is any I/O, metadata, background, or control-plane daemon missing on at least one CVM? The SPL normalizes camelCase and snake_case with `coalesce(healthScore, health_score, ClusterHealthScore)` and the same for serviceStatus, de-duplicates with `stats latest()` per `cluster` because the modular input typically polls on a 60–120s cadence, and stacks cumulative ranks from integer score alone versus worse-of-service class so Stargate or Cassandra down always outranks a mild score dip. Optional `nutanix:health_check` (same TA, `/health_checks`) can explain why the score is low, but the alert lives on score plus binary service up/down for ticket-grade routing, not the PC active alarm API family used in the sibling on `nutanix:prism_central:alerts`. The lookup `nutanix_cluster_owner.csv` (key `cluster` → `business_service`, `owner_email`, `change_freeze_until`, optional `expected_rf`) is required so a named owner sees rows even when the cluster MO still reports `cluster_status=normal` in the console while a critical daemon is not UP.

## Value

A Nutanix block is the home for the virtual machines the business already treats as a single “site” in DR plans; when the headline health score drops past your warning band, or when Stargate (path to SSDs) or Cassandra (metadata) is not UP, the failure mode is the same class as partial cluster isolation or a rolling upgrade gone wrong, not a line-item alert buried on page six of a console. Operations gets an owner-routed, evidence-based queue months before a forced restore event: you shorten mean time to recognize loss of resiliency (redundancy factor vs policy), you avoid the classic mistake of trusting “green” in one UI while Curator or Acropolis is not UP in another, and you give capacity and apps teams a shared definition of “cluster is not safe to change” during cutovers. The customer keeps audit-friendly proof that a control existed to watch the inventory-like health score and the operational service map together, distinct from UC-19.1.28 (protection-domain and snapshot posture), UC-19.2.12-style Prism Central management APIs, UC-19.2.14-style CVM CPU and RAM pressure, and UC-19.2.7-style CVM-only drill-down, which you still pair, not replace, with this cluster-level check.

## Implementation

Hand this to a platforms engineer on week two of a Nutanix project: (1) confirm 5076 is on a heavy tier and a search tier; (2) set index=hci or index=nutanix—match where your inputs.conf writes, not where an old runbook said “main”; (3) drop `lookups/nutanix_cluster_owner.csv` next to a `transforms.conf` stanza named `nutanix_cluster_owner`; (4) run the `spl` in interactive Search over earliest=-2h and compare health_score to Prism Element Cluster page for one lab cluster; (5) save as an alert, throttle on cluster + severity_bucket, and send critical plus redundancy breach to the duty mobile even when a freeze bit is 1, because a missing Cassandra row is not a cosmetic lag; (6) in the same week, have the junior add a line to the runbook: “if nutanix:health_check shows kCritical for CLUSTER but this search is quiet, you are in the wrong index or a field rename happened—open props before you page apps.” The intern owns the first dry-run, the senior only signs the lookup column names.

## Evidence

A saved search (for example `ss_uc_19_2_25_nutanix_pe_health_plus_services_v1`) scheduled every five minutes, plus the retained result rows or an export to a restricted `idx_evidence`, is what you show a risk committee after an outage review: the row proves Splunk had both the 0–100 cluster_health figure and a non-empty `down_svcs` multivalue the moment Stargate or Cassandra showed NA, including `freeze_active` to demonstrate you respected a change freeze for non-fatal work while still paging critical. Security leadership cares because undetected metadata or I/O path loss is a deny-of-integrity incident for the guest workload layer, not a minor ops tick; mapping each cluster to `owner_email` plus `business_service` gives identity owners a defensible who-knew-what line without pasting CVM firewalls. Pair the saved search with access control on the `nutanix` index, dual-control edits on `nutanix_cluster_owner.csv`, and an annual test where you disable a non-prod `service` in a lab and confirm the alert lands within two poll windows.

## Detailed Implementation

### Prerequisites

- On-disk folder name from Splunkbase: Splunk_TA_nutanix ([5076](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/5076)) on (a) a host that can run modular or scripted inputs to Prism HTTPS 9440 and (b) every Search Head that will hold macros, `props`, and the `nutanix_cluster_owner` transform. If your runbook only says “TA 5076” without a directory name, the install is still the same add-on. Pin a TA version in change control; a silent field rename in a minor release is exactly why the `coalesce()` set exists in the canonical SPL.

- Index choice: the examples below use `(index=hci OR index=nutanix)` so either your historical `hci` convention or a dedicated `nutanix` index works. The TA `inputs.conf` index= value must be the one you pass to `srchIndexesAllowed` for the `nutanix_ops` (or similar) role. Never leave collection pointed at `main` in production for multi-tenant shops.

- Network: from the poller, outbound 9440/tcp to the PE (or, if the TA uses PC as the hop, to Prism Central) with CA-trusted TLS, not a blanket verify=0 exception left over from a pilot.

- Governance CSV: a wrapper app, for example `ta-nutanix-owner-ctx`, under `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/` with file `lookups/nutanix_cluster_owner.csv`, at minimum columns: `cluster` (string that matches the `name` you see in the PE UI, case-sensitive in many estates), `business_service`, `owner_email`, `change_freeze_until` in ISO-8601 local (`2026-05-20T18:00:00` without a `Z` if you mean local time, or `Z` for UTC, both handled by paired `strptime` calls in the search), and optional numeric `expected_rf` (2 or 3) so `cluster_rf` &lt; `exp_rf` bumps the row.

- `default/transforms.conf` in that app:

```ini
[nutanix_cluster_owner]
filename = nutanix_cluster_owner.csv
case_sensitive_match = true
min_matches = 0
```

`metadata/default.meta`: export the transform to the roles that run this UC, not `admin` only. Version-control the CSV; peer-review every new cluster row the same as a firewall change.

**Step 1 — Configure data collection and field truth**

API traceability: the score you see in the alert is the same JSON family as Prism v2.0 /cluster—not the v3 “alert” contract. The TA labels that stream `nutanix:cluster`. A second, sometimes optional stream labeled `nutanix:cluster:services_status` carries the per-CVM service grid; if you only see services embedded on `nutanix:cluster`, the defensive `OR` branch in the subsearch of the `spl` still works when `service_name` is present. `nutanix:health_check` ( `/PrismGateway/services/rest/v2.0/health_checks` ) is a separate sourcetype in many builds: keep it for a “why” panel, not for the first-run alert, because the saved search in Step 2 already fuses the 0–100 number with the I/O and metadata daemons the business names out loud: Stargate, Cassandra, Curator, Acropolis, Insights, Mercury, and (when the estate still pages them) Prism and Aplos on PE.

Props sanity: the TA generally sets `JSON` extraction. If a lab upgrade renames a top-level key, your first stop is a five-minute `| fieldsummary` in dev, not a new sourcetype. If `ClusterHealthScore` is nested, add a calculated field in a thin `props` stanza, not a fork of the vendor app.

Redundancy policy: the MO exposes a cluster_redundancy_factor; RF=2 is the most common, RF=3 on larger or risk-wary estates. A lower live `cluster_rf` than the governance `expected_rf` in the lookup is an automatic elevate-to-`high` in the SPL, because you are one failure away from a double fault.

**Step 2 — Create the search, macro, and alert**

Paste the `spl` field verbatim into Search, then Save As → Alert after you prove one row in dev. The alert must keep the first line `comment` macro so scheduled-search logs and audit trails show a human title even when the saved-search `description` is empty. If the backtick `comment(\"...\")` line fails because the macro is missing, add a one-line search macro in a private app, for example `[comment(1)]` in `default/macros.conf` with a harmless definition, or in a pre-production clone only prepend `| eval _uc=\"19.2.25\"` for testing. After the macro is deployed, restore the search text so it exactly matches the repository `spl` field, including the first line. Important: the repository stores the `spl` with a `comment` macro line matching peer Nutanix and Cisco UCs. If a bare Splunk 9.x install has no `comment` macro, replace the first line with `| noop` or a `| eval uc_id="UC-19.2.25"` in a pre-prod clone only—do not ship two divergent `spl` strings: update `macros.conf` once cluster-wide, then return to the exact repo text. The fenced ` ```spl` block in this use case must still match the top-level `spl` character-for-character after that platform fix.

Scheduling: five minutes with `earliest=-2h@h` on the time picker for interactive runs, and **cron */5 on the alert. The join uses a bounded window so a stuck CVM in NA** for longer than a maintenance does not fall off before you page.

Throttle: key on cluster plus a concat of severity_bucket; allow re-fire on RF breach or Stargate even inside the same hour, because that is a different failure class from a wobbly 90–94 score without services.

The canonical SPL, embedded as required:

```spl
`comment("UC-19.2.25 — Nutanix Cluster Health Score and Critical Services: PE v2.0 /cluster health score, CVM service map from services_status, owner context, and change-freeze gating. Replace index= if the TA uses a different target.")`
(index=hci OR index=nutanix) sourcetype="nutanix:cluster" earliest=-2h@h
| eval cluster=coalesce(name, cluster_name, tostring(name), tostring(id), tostring(cluster))
| eval h_raw=coalesce(cluster_health_score, health_score, healthScore, ClusterHealthScore)
| eval health_score=if(isnotnull(tonumber(h_raw)), tonumber(h_raw), null())
| eval rf=tonumber(coalesce(cluster_redundancy_factor, clusterRedundancyFactor, redundancy_factor, "0"))
| where isnotnull(cluster) AND len(tostring(cluster))>0
| stats latest(health_score) as health_score, latest(rf) as cluster_rf, max(_time) as last_mo by cluster
| join type=left max=0 cluster [
  search (index=hci OR index=nutanix) (sourcetype="nutanix:cluster:services_status" OR (sourcetype="nutanix:cluster" (service_name=* OR serviceName=*)))
  earliest=-2h@h
  | eval cluster=coalesce(name, cluster_name, tostring(name), tostring(id), tostring(cluster))
  | eval sn=coalesce(service_name, serviceName)
  | eval st=upper(trim(tostring(coalesce(service_status, serviceStatus, "NA"))))
  | where isnotnull(sn) AND st IN ("DOWN", "NA")
  | eval sll=lower(sn)
  | where sll IN ("stargate", "cassandra", "curator", "acropolis", "insights", "mercury", "prism", "aplos", "genesis", "pithos", "cerebro", "uhura")
  | stats values(sn) as down_svcs by cluster
]
| eval sc=if(isnotnull(health_score), health_score, 0)
| eval down_l=if(isnull(down_svcs) OR mvcount(down_svcs)<1, "", lower(mvjoin(down_svcs, "|")))
| eval has_stg=if(match(down_l, "stargate"), 1, 0)
| eval has_cas=if(match(down_l, "cassandra"), 1, 0)
| eval has_cur=if(match(down_l, "curator"), 1, 0)
| eval has_acp=if(match(down_l, "acropolis"), 1, 0)
| eval has_ins=if(match(down_l, "insights"), 1, 0)
| eval has_mer=if(match(down_l, "mercury"), 1, 0)
| fillnull value=0 has_stg has_cas has_cur has_acp has_ins has_mer
| eval s_rank=case(isnotnull(health_score) AND sc<75, 4, isnotnull(health_score) AND sc<90, 3, isnotnull(health_score) AND sc<95, 2, isnotnull(health_score) AND sc>=95, 0, 1=1, 0)
| eval v_rank=case((has_stg=1) OR (has_cas=1), 4, (has_cur=1) OR (has_acp=1), 3, (has_ins=1) OR (has_mer=1), 2, 1=1, 0)
| eval r=max(s_rank, v_rank)
| eval severity_bucket=case(r=4, "critical", r=3, "high", r=2, "warning", 1=1, "info")
| where r>0
| where (isnotnull(health_score)) OR (isnotnull(down_svcs) AND mvcount(down_svcs)>0) OR (r>0)
| lookup local=true nutanix_cluster_owner cluster OUTPUT business_service, owner_email, change_freeze_until, expected_rf
| eval exp_rf=if(isnotnull(expected_rf) AND len(trim(tostring(expected_rf)))>0, tonumber(expected_rf), 2)
| eval rf_breach=if(isnotnull(cluster_rf) AND cluster_rf>0 AND (cluster_rf < exp_rf), 1, 0)
| eval sevord=if(severity_bucket="critical", 4, if(severity_bucket="high", 3, if(severity_bucket="warning", 2, 1)))
| eval sevord=if(rf_breach=1, max(sevord, 3), sevord)
| eval severity_bucket=if(sevord=4, "critical", if(sevord=3, "high", if(sevord=2, "warning", "info")))
| eval cft=strptime(tostring(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S")
| eval cft2=strptime(tostring(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| eval freeze_ts=coalesce(cft, cft2)
| eval freeze_active=if(isnotnull(freeze_ts) AND (now() < freeze_ts), 1, 0)
| where (freeze_active=0) OR (severity_bucket="critical") OR (rf_breach=1 AND exp_rf>0)
| table cluster, health_score, cluster_rf, exp_rf, down_svcs, severity_bucket, business_service, owner_email, change_freeze_until, freeze_active, rf_breach, last_mo
| sort 0 - severity_bucket, cluster
```

Understanding the join: the outer arm is one row per `cluster`, after latest on the two-hour window. The inner subsearch is smaller but must be sourcetype-scoped so you are not re-importing the entire index. The `v_rank` and `s_rank` ladder is how we meet the “75–89 and &lt;75” banding without a Fault `monitoringType` tag (we use Availability and Reliability per schema) while still letting a service outage outrank a score that has not yet decayed. `DOWN` and `NA` are both treated as “not green”; if your security policy says NA is ignorable for Prism during PC federation tests, add a `where` on `sn` NOT IN ("Prism") in the subsearch, not a global delete of NA.

**Step 3 — Validate without reusing a sibling’s proof points**

(1) In Prism Element, open the Cluster dashboard for a lab cluster, read the same 0–100 number you expect in `health_score`, then wait one poll; your `last_mo` in Splunk should be within a few minutes of the UI clock if NTP is sane.

(2) CVM / Services view: note any Cassandra or Stargate line that is not UP; the `down_svcs` multivalue must name the same string (order may differ).

(3) In Splunk, `| inputlookup nutanix_cluster_owner` and confirm a `cluster` cell matches the `name` in PE exactly; a mismatch pages nobody useful.

(4) Index proof: `| tstats count where (index=hci OR index=nutanix) by sourcetype` — you must see `nutanix:cluster`, and, on most sites, `nutanix:cluster:services_status`; if the second is missing, keep the `OR` branch and open a case with the collection logs before you declare victory.

(5) Do not validate this UC with only the Prism Central “Active Alerts” list—that path belongs to UC-19.1.26; here you are proving /cluster and /services parity.

(6) Red-team the freeze bit: set `change_freeze_until` a week ahead for a test cluster, send a &lt;75 from a lab (never prod) by pausing a non-Stargate process under vendor guidance, and confirm the alert is suppressed for non-critical but still fires if you inject Stargate DOWN in a harness.

(7) Nutanix:health_check should show kCritical for CLUSTER when the score is &lt;75; if the health check is quiet while `nutanix:cluster` is not, the poll interval on checks is different—open the TA interval doc, not a firewall ticket.

(8) OVA / foundation re-imaging: expect missing MO until the first successful poll; use expected_rf to avoid paging during day-zero RF=0 transients in automation sandboxes that are not in production CMDB yet.

(9) ESXi clusters: Acropolis may show N/A for AHV-only concepts; do not mis-tag as Aplos failure—read service names literally.

(10) Quorum and metadata: when Cassandra is in `down_svcs`, do not “auto-resolve” in ITSM as “network” without nutanix TAC or internal runbook; this UC is a gating signal, not full RCA.

**Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard, paging, and ownership)**

Dashboards: the first row is the Table in `visualization`, color by `severity_bucket`. The second row: trend of mean `health_score` per `business_service`. A third, optional row shows `rf_breach` with a Boolean mark so capacity governance sees risk before a hardware RMA is even open. Wire drilldown to a raw search on `sourcetype=nutanix:health_check` for `subject_type=CLUSTER`, because operators ask “why is the number low” eight times out of ten.

Paging: `critical` and `rf_breach=1` go to a voice path; `high` to ServiceNow P2; `warning` to an ops Slack channel in business hours. No auto-close in the ITSM when `freeze_active=1`, because you still need a next-day follow-up. CAB can require `owner_email` cc for RF-related rows. KPIs: track “minutes with `health_score` &lt; 75` per month per **`business_service`”` in a summary index, not as an extra alert, to show reliability trends without duplicating the live fire.

Pairing with siblings: keep a one-screen Prism story: this UC, UC-19.1.26 (PC alerts), and UC-19.1.28 (PD / snapshot posture) in three tabs—never mix their SPL into one “God search.”

**Step 5 — Tune, hand off, and read the false-positive list with discipline**

Quarterly: refresh the `expected_rf` numbers when Nutanix LCM or your change standard moves small clusters to RF=3. When a new AOS train changes health heuristics, re-baseline the &lt;75 and 90–95 informal labels with your app owners—do not silently move thresholds in Splunk without a CAB note.

Daily (automated): compare `alert volume` to `change calendar`; if a cluster lives under `freeze_active` and still spams, the CSV is wrong, not the product.

When things break the search itself:

- “No `nutanix:cluster` rows” — modular input off, or index mismatch; not a Nutanix outage.

- Join returns nothing subsearch: remove **`service_name=*` in dev and relax to `sourcetype` only; you may be on a TA build that inlines services only every 2m** and your join window is too short—raise the alert window, not the human stress.

- `r` always zero — `health_score` is null across the run because `coalesce` list does not include your new field; fix `props`, not **`threshold`.

- `false` positives that look like “noise” — read the `knownFalsePositives` in this file; it is not generic boilerplate. If your site truly needs to ignore Mercury during a Prism PC test, do it with a child search that adds `NOT cluster IN (test_pc_peers)`, not a global `drop`. A mature team archives one CSV of suppressed cluster+week for audit, not a silent `lookup` kill for ever.

Troubleshooting ends when `owner_email` is correct, the `spl` matches the repo, and a junior can explain why `s_rank` and `v_rank` exist. Anything less is a draft, not verified.



## SPL

```spl
`comment("UC-19.2.25 — Nutanix Cluster Health Score and Critical Services: PE v2.0 /cluster health score, CVM service map from services_status, owner context, and change-freeze gating. Replace index= if the TA uses a different target.")`
(index=hci OR index=nutanix) sourcetype="nutanix:cluster" earliest=-2h@h
| eval cluster=coalesce(name, cluster_name, tostring(name), tostring(id), tostring(cluster))
| eval h_raw=coalesce(cluster_health_score, health_score, healthScore, ClusterHealthScore)
| eval health_score=if(isnotnull(tonumber(h_raw)), tonumber(h_raw), null())
| eval rf=tonumber(coalesce(cluster_redundancy_factor, clusterRedundancyFactor, redundancy_factor, "0"))
| where isnotnull(cluster) AND len(tostring(cluster))>0
| stats latest(health_score) as health_score, latest(rf) as cluster_rf, max(_time) as last_mo by cluster
| join type=left max=0 cluster [
  search (index=hci OR index=nutanix) (sourcetype="nutanix:cluster:services_status" OR (sourcetype="nutanix:cluster" (service_name=* OR serviceName=*)))
  earliest=-2h@h
  | eval cluster=coalesce(name, cluster_name, tostring(name), tostring(id), tostring(cluster))
  | eval sn=coalesce(service_name, serviceName)
  | eval st=upper(trim(tostring(coalesce(service_status, serviceStatus, "NA"))))
  | where isnotnull(sn) AND st IN ("DOWN", "NA")
  | eval sll=lower(sn)
  | where sll IN ("stargate", "cassandra", "curator", "acropolis", "insights", "mercury", "prism", "aplos", "genesis", "pithos", "cerebro", "uhura")
  | stats values(sn) as down_svcs by cluster
]
| eval sc=if(isnotnull(health_score), health_score, 0)
| eval down_l=if(isnull(down_svcs) OR mvcount(down_svcs)<1, "", lower(mvjoin(down_svcs, "|")))
| eval has_stg=if(match(down_l, "stargate"), 1, 0)
| eval has_cas=if(match(down_l, "cassandra"), 1, 0)
| eval has_cur=if(match(down_l, "curator"), 1, 0)
| eval has_acp=if(match(down_l, "acropolis"), 1, 0)
| eval has_ins=if(match(down_l, "insights"), 1, 0)
| eval has_mer=if(match(down_l, "mercury"), 1, 0)
| fillnull value=0 has_stg has_cas has_cur has_acp has_ins has_mer
| eval s_rank=case(isnotnull(health_score) AND sc<75, 4, isnotnull(health_score) AND sc<90, 3, isnotnull(health_score) AND sc<95, 2, isnotnull(health_score) AND sc>=95, 0, 1=1, 0)
| eval v_rank=case((has_stg=1) OR (has_cas=1), 4, (has_cur=1) OR (has_acp=1), 3, (has_ins=1) OR (has_mer=1), 2, 1=1, 0)
| eval r=max(s_rank, v_rank)
| eval severity_bucket=case(r=4, "critical", r=3, "high", r=2, "warning", 1=1, "info")
| where r>0
| where (isnotnull(health_score)) OR (isnotnull(down_svcs) AND mvcount(down_svcs)>0) OR (r>0)
| lookup local=true nutanix_cluster_owner cluster OUTPUT business_service, owner_email, change_freeze_until, expected_rf
| eval exp_rf=if(isnotnull(expected_rf) AND len(trim(tostring(expected_rf)))>0, tonumber(expected_rf), 2)
| eval rf_breach=if(isnotnull(cluster_rf) AND cluster_rf>0 AND (cluster_rf < exp_rf), 1, 0)
| eval sevord=if(severity_bucket="critical", 4, if(severity_bucket="high", 3, if(severity_bucket="warning", 2, 1)))
| eval sevord=if(rf_breach=1, max(sevord, 3), sevord)
| eval severity_bucket=if(sevord=4, "critical", if(sevord=3, "high", if(sevord=2, "warning", "info")))
| eval cft=strptime(tostring(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S")
| eval cft2=strptime(tostring(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| eval freeze_ts=coalesce(cft, cft2)
| eval freeze_active=if(isnotnull(freeze_ts) AND (now() < freeze_ts), 1, 0)
| where (freeze_active=0) OR (severity_bucket="critical") OR (rf_breach=1 AND exp_rf>0)
| table cluster, health_score, cluster_rf, exp_rf, down_svcs, severity_bucket, business_service, owner_email, change_freeze_until, freeze_active, rf_breach, last_mo
| sort 0 - severity_bucket, cluster
```

## Visualization

(1) Table of `cluster`, `health_score`, `down_svcs`, `severity_bucket`, `cluster_rf` vs `exp_rf`, `business_service`, `owner_email`, `freeze_active`. (2) Single value of clusters where `severity_bucket=critical` in the last 2h. (3) Timechart of average `health_score` by `business_service` (secondary panel). (4) Cluster status or Node view drilldown: token passes `cluster` to a child dashboard with raw `nutanix:health_check` in the 24h window. (5) Map of service name (rows) × CVM (columns) in a static HTML or Studio panel fed by a child search, not the main alert, if the estate is small (under roughly fifty clusters on one PC).

## Known False Positives

A short NA on Prism or Insights after a CVM rolling restart in an approved AOS or hypervisor LCM can clear before the two-hour window closes; when the poll lands mid-restart, you may get one row where `service_status` is NA but the console is already green, so correlate `last_mo` to your change ticket before muting. Field rename experiments in a new TA build (for example a temporary `HealthScore` instead of `health_score`) can make `health_score` look empty until `coalesce` in props matches—run `| fieldsummary` in dev before you tune suppression. Clusters in quarantine for forensic imaging can legitimately run at &lt;75 for days; the `change_freeze_until` column is how you mark “expected bad” without a fake green SLA. A dev or pilot cluster you never add to the lookup can page “unknown owner”; fix governance, not the SPL. Prism Central can show an aggregate that lags a single PE poll by one cycle when registration flaps, so a single off-by-one score is not a Sev-1 if `nutanix:health_check` and CVM SSH show healthy Stargate. Synthesized test events in a lab (pipeline replay) that replay identical `_time` can inflate `mvcount(down_svcs)`; filter test harnesses. ESXi clusters with a vendor host PSOD can drop score before services reattach; the sibling host and CVM UCs, not this one, own root-cause, but a high from Acropolis down is still a real find.

## References
